RULE 4: When the stride register rs2 of VLSE32.V is x0, an implementation may perform fewer memory operations than the number of active elements, but it is not required to do so.
RULE 5: Different dynamic executions of the same static VLSE32.V instruction may perform different numbers of memory operations when rs2 is x0.
RULE 6: Compilers must avoid emitting VLSE32.V with rs2 = x0 when the intended stride is zero and the program requires that all memory accesses actually occur.
RULE 7: When rs2 is not x0 and the value read from the register specified by rs2 is zero, VLSE32.V must perform one memory access for each active element, still unordered.
RULE 8: VLSE32.V must appear to perform each memory access; microarchitectures may only optimise away accesses that cannot be observed, such as in idempotent memory regions obeying RVWMO.
RULE 9: VLSE32.V may not optimise away accesses in non‑idempotent memory regions because each access could be observed by a device.
RULE 10: When ordered accesses to the same memory address are required, an ordered indexed operation should be used instead of VLSE32.V.

INSTRUCTION: VLSE64.V
RULE 1: VLSE64.V is a 64‑bit strided load that takes a destination vector register vd, a base address register rs1, a stride register rs2, and a mask register vm.
RULE 2: VLSE64.V supports negative stride values.
RULE 3: VLSE64.V supports a zero stride value.
RULE 4: Element accesses performed by VLSE64.V are unordered with respect to each other.
RULE 5: If the stride register rs2 of VLSE64.V is x0, an implementation may perform fewer memory operations than the number of active elements.
RULE 6: When rs2 is x0 in VLSE64.V, an implementation is not required to reduce the number of memory operations.
RULE 7: A VLSE64.V instruction with rs2 = x0 may perform a different number of memory operations across different dynamic executions of the same static instruction.
RULE 8: Compilers must avoid using x0 for rs2 in VLSE64.V when the programmer intends a static zero stride that guarantees all memory accesses are performed.
RULE 9: If rs2 is not x0 in VLSE64.V and the value held in the register indicated by rs2 equals zero, the implementation must perform one memory access for each active element.
RULE 10: The memory accesses required by VLSE64.V when rs2 is non‑zero but its value is zero remain unordered.
RULE 11: Implementations of VLSE64.V must appear to perform each memory access required by the instruction.
RULE 12: A microarchitecture may optimize away VLSE64.V memory accesses that are not observable by another agent if the accessed memory region is idempotent and the optimization obeys the RVWMO memory‑ordering model.
RULE 13: Optimizations that remove VLSE64.V memory accesses are prohibited for non‑idempotent memory regions such as memory‑mapped I/O because each access must be observable.
RULE 14: When ordered repeated vector accesses to the same memory address are required, an ordered indexed operation must be used instead of VLSE64.V.

INSTRUCTION: VSE8.V
RULE 1: VSE8.V stores the 8‑bit elements contained in the source vector register group vs3 to memory.
RULE 2: VSE8.V uses the integer register rs1 as the base address from which the first byte is stored.
RULE 3: VSE8.V stores each vector element as a single byte.
RULE 4: VSE8.V writes the bytes to consecutive memory locations with a stride of one byte between stored elements.
RULE 5: VSE8.V applies element‑wise masking controlled by the mask operand vm, and elements whose mask bit is false are not written to memory.
RULE 6: VSE8.V treats the mask operand vm as either a mask register such as v0.t or as omitted, which implies no masking.
RULE 7: VSE8.V uses the width[2:0] field value 0, the same encoding used by VLE8.V.
RULE 8: VSE8.V is distinguished from other vector memory instructions by its specific sumop encoding.
RULE 9: VSE8.V stores 8‑bit elements from the specified source vector register to memory at the address contained in the given scalar register.
RULE 10: When VSE8.V includes a mask operand written as <mask>.t, only the elements whose corresponding mask bit is 1 are written to memory and elements with mask bit 0 remain unchanged.
RULE 11: If VSE8.V is issued without an explicit mask operand, it stores all elements of the source vector register to memory (unmasked store).
RULE 12: The element type processed by VSE8.V is a byte (8‑bit) as indicated by the e8 element width.
RULE 13: The memory address used by VSE8.V is taken from the scalar register operand enclosed in parentheses and is not altered by the instruction.
RULE 14: VSE8.V does not modify the scalar register that supplies the base address; any pointer increment must be performed by separate instructions.
RULE 15: The mask operand syntax for VSE8.V in the given description is limited to the true‑masked form .t; no false‑masked (.f) form is described.
RULE 16: When VSE8.V is masked, the store operation writes only the masked‑true elements and leaves the contents of unmasked memory locations unchanged.
RULE 17: No architectural constraints such as alignment requirements, exception behavior, or write‑mask interactions are specified for VSE8.V in the provided text.

INSTRUCTION: VSE16.V
RULE 1: VSE16.V stores 16‑bit elements from the source vector register group vs3 to memory.
RULE 2: VSE16.V uses the integer register rs1 as the base address for the first stored element.
RULE 3: VSE16.V writes each successive 16‑bit element to the next sequential 16‑bit memory address, i.e., with a unit stride of two bytes.
RULE 4: VSE16.V may include an optional mask vm; when the mask is present, only elements whose corresponding mask bit is 1 are written to memory.
RULE 5: VSE16.V leaves memory unchanged for any element whose corresponding mask bit is 0 when a mask is present.
RULE 6: If the mask operand vm is omitted from VSE16.V, the store is performed unmasked, writing all active elements.
RULE 7: The mask operand vm for VSE16.V is encoded as v0.t when a mask is used.
RULE 8: VSE16.V is classified as a vector unit‑stride store instruction alongside VSE8.V, VSE32.V, and VSE64.V.
RULE 9: The source register group vs3 for VSE16.V must contain at least as many elements as the current vector length.
RULE 10: VSE16.V operates on the active vector elements as defined by the current VL and VLMAX settings.

INSTRUCTION: VSE32.V
RULE 1: VSE32.V stores a vector register group beginning at vector register vd to memory using the address in integer register rs1 as the base address.
RULE 2: VSE32.V is defined only when the current selected element width (SEW) is 32 bits; otherwise it raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 3: VSE32.V stores exactly the first VL elements of the source vector register group, where VL is the current vector length set by the most recent vsetvli instruction.
RULE 4: VSE32.V requires that the vector register group width (LMUL) be compatible with the current LMUL setting, and the instruction implicitly operates on the contiguous registers covering that group.
RULE 5: For each element index i from 0 to VL‑1, VSE32.V computes the effective memory address as rs1 plus i times 4 bytes.
RULE 6: VSE32.V does not modify the base register rs1; the register retains its original value after the store.
RULE 7: VSE32.V requires that the base address rs1 be 4‑byte aligned, and a mis‑aligned address causes a mis‑aligned‑access exception.
RULE 8: If any effective address computed by VSE32.V falls outside the accessible memory region, a page‑fault or access‑fault exception is raised.
RULE 9: When VSE32.V executes without exception, the memory locations beginning at the address in rs1 contain the first VL 32‑bit elements of the source vector register group in order of increasing element index.
RULE 10: VSE32.V does not perform any automatic address post‑increment; any increment of rs1 must be done explicitly by the programmer.
RULE 11: VSE32.V stores each active element of the source vector register group vs3 as a 32‑bit word to memory.
RULE 12: VSE32.V computes the effective address for element i as the value in rs1 plus i multiplied by 4 bytes.
RULE 13: VSE32.V uses a unit‑stride of 4 bytes, so successive stored elements are placed at memory addresses separated by exactly 4 bytes.
RULE 14: VSE32.V includes a mask operand vm that determines element activity; a clear mask bit prevents that element from being written.
RULE 15: VSE32.V leaves the memory location unchanged for any element whose mask bit is clear.
RULE 16: VSE32.V writes only those elements whose mask vm bits are set, storing them at consecutive addresses defined by the unit‑stride.
RULE 17: VSE32.V reads the data to be stored from the vector register group vs3 specified as its source operand.

INSTRUCTION: VSE64.V
RULE 1: VSE64.V stores 64‑bit elements from the vector register group specified by vs3 to memory.
RULE 2: VSE64.V uses the integer register rs1 as the base address and writes the first element to the address contained in rs1.
RULE 3: VSE64.V writes successive elements to memory addresses calculated as rs1 + i*8 for element index i ranging from 0 to VL‑1.
RULE 4: VSE64.V writes each element as a 64‑bit unit, giving the instruction a unit‑stride of 8 bytes.
RULE 5: VSE64.V may include an optional mask operand vm that controls which elements are written.
RULE 6: VSE64.V stores an element only when the corresponding mask bit in vm is true, and it does not store the element when that mask bit is false.
RULE 7: VSE64.V stores all elements unconditionally when the mask operand vm is omitted.
RULE 8: VSE64.V encodes the optional mask operand vm as the vector register v0.t when it is present.

INSTRUCTION: VSM.V
RULE 1: VSM.V stores a byte‑wide vector to memory whose effective length (EVL) equals the ceiling of the architectural vector length divided by eight (⌈VL/8⌉).
RULE 2: VSM.V writes each stored byte as a packed representation of eight mask bits derived from the source vector register.
RULE 3: VSM.V performs the store using unit‑stride addressing, writing consecutive memory locations starting at the address in rs1.
RULE 4: VSM.V operates with an EMUL value of 1, meaning its elements are byte‑wide.
RULE 5: VSM.V interprets the vstart register in units of bytes, so when vstart = k the store begins at the k‑th byte of the source vector.
RULE 6: VSM.V respects the vill field of vtype, and any illegal vtype configuration that makes vl illegal also makes the VSM.V instruction illegal.
RULE 7: The EVL computed for VSM.V depends on vtype‑imposed constraints that affect VL, and VSM.V uses that EVL to determine how many bytes are written.
RULE 8: VSM.V writes the packed mask vector to memory up to EVL without applying a tail‑agnostic or agnostic policy; the store writes the full set of EVL bytes.
RULE 9: VSM.V reads its source vector register (vs3) as the source of the mask bits that are packed into the stored bytes.

INSTRUCTION: VSSE8.V
RULE 1: VSSE8.V is a vector‑strided store that writes 8‑bit elements from a source vector register group to memory.
RULE 2: VSSE8.V uses the register rs1 as a base address, the register rs2 as a byte‑stride value, and the mask register vm to select active elements for storing.
RULE 3: VSSE8.V stores one 8‑bit element for each active element in the source vector register group at an address equal to the base address plus the byte‑stride value in rs2 multiplied by the element index.
RULE 4: VSSE8.V supports negative and zero stride values in rs2.
RULE 5: VSSE8.V performs the memory writes for the active elements in an unordered fashion, with no defined order among the individual stores.
RULE 6: When rs2 is the zero register (x0), VSSE8.V may perform fewer memory operations than the number of active elements.
RULE 7: When rs2 is the zero register (x0), VSSE8.V is not required to perform a fixed number of memory operations, and the actual number may differ between dynamic executions of the same static instruction.
RULE 8: Compilers must avoid using rs2 = x0 for VSSE8.V when an immediate stride of zero is required and the programmer needs every memory access to be performed.
RULE 9: When rs2 is not x0 and the memory location addressed by rs2 contains a zero stride value, VSSE8.V must perform one memory access for each active element, though the accesses remain unordered.
RULE 10: VSSE8.V must ensure that each memory access appears to have been performed, even if the implementation internally optimizes away accesses that cannot be observed.
RULE 11: VSSE8.V may internally eliminate memory accesses that are unobservable in idempotent memory regions that obey the RVWMO memory model.
RULE 12: VSSE8.V may not eliminate memory accesses in non‑idempotent memory regions where each access can be observed by a device.
RULE 13: If ordered repeated accesses to the same memory address are required, VSSE8.V should not be used; instead an ordered indexed operation must be employed.

INSTRUCTION: VSSE16.V
RULE 1: VSSE16.V stores 16‑bit elements from the source vector register group vs3 to memory addresses computed as the base address in rs1 plus the element index multiplied by the byte stride held in rs2, applying the optional mask vm.
RULE 2: VSSE16.V permits the stride value held in rs2 to be negative, zero, or positive.
RULE 3: The memory accesses generated by a single execution of VSSE16.V are unordered with respect to one another.
RULE 4: When rs2 is register x0, indicating a zero‑stride for VSSE16.V, the implementation may perform fewer memory operations than the number of active elements, but it is not required to do so.
RULE 5: Dynamic executions of the same static VSSE16.V instruction may perform different numbers of memory operations when rs2 is x0.
RULE 6: Compilers must avoid encoding VSSE16.V with rs2 = x0 when the programmer’s intent is that all memory accesses be performed for a zero‑stride case.
RULE 7: When rs2 is not x0 and the stride value read from the register specified by rs2 equals zero, VSSE16.V must perform one memory access for each active element, with the accesses remaining unordered.
RULE 8: Implementations of VSSE16.V must appear to perform each memory access defined by the architectural specification.
RULE 9: A microarchitecture may optimise away VSSE16.V memory accesses that cannot be observed by another agent, such as accesses to idempotent memory regions under RVWMO.
RULE 10: Optimisation that removes VSSE16.V memory accesses is prohibited for non‑idempotent memory regions where each access can be observed by a device.
RULE 11: If an ordered sequence of repeated vector accesses to the same memory address is required, VSSE16.V must not be used; an ordered indexed operation should be employed instead.

INSTRUCTION: VSSE32.V
RULE 1: VSSE32.V stores 32‑bit elements from a source vector register group to memory using a base address in a scalar integer register and a signed byte stride from another scalar integer register, optionally masked by a mask register.
RULE 2: VSSE32.V requires the source vector register group to contain 32‑bit elements.
RULE 3: VSSE32.V uses rs1 as a scalar integer register that holds the base address for the store.
RULE 4: VSSE32.V uses rs2 as a scalar integer register that holds the signed byte stride for the store.
RULE 5: VSSE32.V uses vm as a mask register that determines which vector elements are active for the store.
RULE 6: VSSE32.V supports positive, negative, and zero byte strides.
RULE 7: VSSE32.V does not guarantee any ordering between the memory accesses generated for different vector elements; the element accesses are unordered.
RULE 8: When rs2 is the zero register (x0), VSSE32.V may perform fewer memory operations than the number of active elements, but it is not required to do so.
RULE 9: When rs2 is the zero register (x0), the number of memory operations performed by VSSE32.V may differ between dynamic executions of the same static instruction.
RULE 10: Compilers must avoid emitting VSSE32.V with rs2 = x0 when the programmer's intent is to force a store for every active element.
RULE 11: When rs2 is not the zero register and the runtime value in the indicated scalar register is zero, VSSE32.V must perform one memory access for each active element, although the accesses remain unordered.
RULE 12: Implementations of VSSE32.V must appear to perform each memory access specified by the instruction.
RULE 13: Microarchitectures may optimize away VSSE32.V accesses that are not observable by another agent in idempotent memory regions that obey the RVWMO memory model.
RULE 14: In non‑idempotent memory regions such as memory‑mapped I/O, microarchitectures must not optimize away any VSSE32.V stores; every store must be performed.
RULE 15: If ordered repeated stores to the same memory address are required, programmers should use an ordered indexed vector operation instead of VSSE32.V.
